---
title: "Problem Set 3"
author: "Xénia Alemany, Michael Fehl"
date: "March 10, 2026"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    geometry: margin=1in
---

# Cascades and Network Structure

## Question 1

### (a) Planted Partition

**(a)** The "planted partition" model we saw in class is a one-parameter version of the stochastic block model that generates simple synthetic networks with community structure of varying strength. Generate and create simple visualizations of three graphs, for $n = 100$ (population size), $c = 2$ (number of communities), $d = 10$ (average degree), and $\varepsilon \in \{0, 4, 8\}$ (strength of communities as shown in the slides).

Recall that in the slides we saw how $\varepsilon$ maps into the probabilities $p$ (within-group) and $q$ (across-group). Comment on the strength of the community structure each of these graphs exhibits.

\vspace{6cm}

### (b) SI Cascade Model

**(b)** A simple SI spreading process is one in which the probability that a vertex in the infected state $I$ transmits its infection to a particular uninfected neighbor is a constant $p$, and we evaluate that transmission possibility at most once for that edge over the lifetime of the epidemic. Initially, at time $t = 0$, all vertices are in the uninfected state $S$ ("susceptible"), time proceeds in discrete steps and vertex state updates are applied simultaneously when moving from $t \rightarrow t+1$. A simulated epidemic is deemed complete when no new infected nodes are produced in a time step. The epidemic's size $s$ is the fraction of nodes in the $I$ state when the epidemic is complete, and its length $\ell$ is the time $t = \ell$ at which the last node in the epidemic became infected. To begin the epidemic, at time $t = 1$, choose a node uniformly at random to infect.

Using the planted partition model from part (a), you can generate any number of synthetic networks to study the behavior of the simple Independent Cascade model in the slides. Using $n = 1000$, $d = 8$, and $\varepsilon = 0$ measure the average epidemic size $\langle s/n \rangle$ and average epidemic length $\langle \ell \rangle$ as a function of $p \in [0,1]$. Make two figures showing these measured relationships.

Comment on the qualitative behavior of these measured relationships as a function of the transmission probability $p$, and discuss your results relative to your expectations. Is there a critical value of $p$ where a phase transition occurs?

\vspace{6cm}

### (c) Community Structure & Epidemic Size

**(c)** Now investigate whether the strength of community structure enhances, limits, or has no effect on epidemic size. Let $n = 200$ and $d = 8$, and consider various combinations of the two parameters: $p \in [0,1]$ and $\varepsilon \in [0, 2c]$.

Present your characterization of how community structure strength $\varepsilon$ impacts epidemic size $s/n$ and/or epidemic length $\ell$ using one or more figures that show the relationship clearly. Discuss the qualitative shape of these functions, and how they contrast, if at all, with your results from part (1b). Provide a brief intuitive explanation for why community structure strength does or does not impact the shape of the epidemic.

\vspace{6cm}

---

# Optimal Seeding

## Question 2

### (a) Diffusion Simulations: MC

**(a)** For $\lambda = 1$ and $\lambda = 2$, conduct technology diffusion simulations for your village, where each individual draws an adoption threshold $\tau$ from a $\mathcal{N}(\lambda, 0.5)$, but truncated to be strictly positive. In the simulations, when a node is connected to at least $\tau$ individuals who are informed, she becomes informed in the next period. Run the model for four periods. Given the randomness built into the model, simulate the model 60 times for each potential pair of seeds, and create a measure of the average information rate induced by each pair.

\vspace{6cm}

### (b) Optimal Seeds

**(b)** Provide the identity of the optimal pair of seeds under $\lambda = 1$ and $\lambda = 2$.

\vspace{4cm}

### (c) Random Seeds: MC Sim

**(c)** Now run the same Monte Carlo simulation as in part (a) but using randomly chosen seeds. Compare the rates of information diffusion with the optimal seeds in part (a) and discuss the results.

\vspace{6cm}

---

# Routing on Roads

## Question 3

Consider the traffic flow game pictured below. There are two origin-destination pairs. A unit of traffic needs to flow from the upper left to the upper right, and another unit needs to flow from the lower left to the lower right. The cost functions are given in the figure.

\vspace{1cm}

### (a) Socially Optimal Route

**(a)** What is the socially optimal routing, and what is its total cost?

\vspace{5cm}

### (b) Equilibrium Routing

**(b)** What is the equilibrium routing? What is the welfare loss relative to the optimum?

\vspace{5cm}

### (c) Tolls and Subsidies

**(c)** Suppose you can impose constant tolls on some edges and constant subsidies on others. Design a system of tolls and subsidies to implement the social optimum as an equilibrium.

\vspace{5cm}

## Question 4 *(Extra Points)*

### (a) Download & Plot Data

**(a)** Download one of the available road networks and import the data. Plot your network.

\vspace{4cm}

### (b) Compute NE of Network

**(b)** Compute the equilibrium (i.e. Nash) flow of your network by minimizing the potential function that we saw in class. The latency function is:

$$l_{ij} = \frac{d_{ij}}{v_{ij}} \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( \frac{x_{ij}}{p_{ij}} \right)^\beta \right]$$

where $\alpha = 0.2$, $\beta = 10$, and $v_{ij} = 60$ kph for all links.

\vspace{4cm}

### (c) Cost Comparison

**(c)** Compare the costs of the Nash flow on the original network with those on networks where one street is closed to traffic. Repeat by closing each street one at a time.

\vspace{4cm}

### (d) Street Closing Identification

**(d)** Can you find street(s) where closing it decreased the cost of the Nash flow?

\vspace{4cm}

---

# Opinion Dynamics

## Question 5

We assume that each node "listens" to all her neighbors (and herself) with equal probability: $T_{ij} = \frac{1}{1 + d_i}$ for all $j \in N_i$, and $T_{ii} = \frac{1}{1 + d_i}$.

Assign to each node a $p_i(0)$ drawn from a Uniform$(0,1)$.

### (a-b) Data, Matrices, and Opinion Simulations

**(a–b)** Download the Facebook100 data. Keep the largest component of each network only. Set up the transition matrix and simulate innate opinions as described above.

\vspace{4cm}

### (c) Influence of Networks

**(c)** Compute the influence vector for each network and report a histogram of the influences obtained across all vectors. What is the maximum influence? What is the median influence?

\vspace{5cm}

### (d) Consensus Opinions

**(d)** Plot a histogram of the consensus opinions across all networks. Which is the most popular (modal) opinion?

\vspace{5cm}

### (e) Calculate (...)

**(e)** For each network, calculate the first period $t^*$ where:

$$\left( \sum_i \tilde{d}_i(A) \left( p_i(t) - p_i(\infty) \right)^2 \right)^{1/2} < \varepsilon$$

for $\varepsilon = 0.1$ and $\tilde{d}_i(A) = \frac{d_i(A)}{\sum_i d_i(A)}$.

\vspace{4cm}

### (f) Second Eigenvalue Plot

**(f)** Construct a plot with the second eigenvalue of the network on the $x$-axis and $t^*$ on the $y$-axis. Do you get the relationship you expected? What happens as you lower the value of $\varepsilon$?

\vspace{5cm}

### (g) Susceptibility Parameter

**(g)** *(Extra Points)* Now assume that the $p_i(0)$ obtained from the uniform distribution is in fact the innate opinion $s_i$ of agent $i$. Simulate a susceptibility parameter $\alpha_i \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$ for each agent $i$ and solve, for each network, the steady-state value $p^*$ under the Friedkin-Johnsen model. Construct a histogram of $p^*$ across all networks. What is the most popular opinion? How does your answer change if $\alpha_i \sim \text{Uniform}(0.5, 1)$?

\vspace{5cm}

### (h) Optimal Vector

**(h)** *(Extra Points)* Pick your favorite network from Facebook100 and find the optimal vector $\alpha^*$ that maximizes the sum of opinions $\mathbf{1}^\top p^*$ as in Abebe et al. (2018).

\vspace{5cm}
